articles.title: Beeg float library, a Rust port of Fabrice Bellard's libbf
articles.summary: The Beeg float library is a Rust port of Fabrice Bellard's libbf, a tiny arbitrary-precision floating-point library that provides full IEEE 754 semantics and transcendental functions for decimal floating point arithmetic. This library is notable for its performance, which is comparable to the C original but with constant-factor overhead from Rust's bounds checking and allocation model.

articles.title: Mouse: Precision Editing Tools for AI Coding Agents
articles.summary: HIC Mouse is a powerful AI agent file-editing tool that transforms how AI agents work with files, offering precision editing capabilities through coordinate-based editing, staged changes with atomic rollback, and embedded guidance in all responses. This technology enables AI agents to make proposed changes before saving or rolling back changes cleanly, providing a more accurate and reliable editing experience. By addressing common limitations of existing AI file editing tools, HIC Mouse aims to improve the consistency and accuracy of AI coding agents.

articles.summary: Moby Dick Workout is a productivity tool that tests how well an app handles user-generated content, such as writing and editing. The test involves opening the app, resizing windows to various sizes, scrolling through content, selecting all text, cutting and pasting, undoing, and redoing actions without experiencing typing lag or scroll jumping. If the app passes this test, it suggests that it can handle a user's needs efficiently.

articles.title: Scheme is a Hoot
articles.summary: Scheme is a powerful programming language that allows developers to create complex applications by compiling them to the Web using Hoot, a tool that enables Guile code to run on native platforms and WASM. While initially unstable, Hoot has been improved with new versions, making it easier for developers to implement their ideas in a more reliable way. As Scheme's capabilities expand into areas like physics, such as its recent success in compiling Zig + Scheme programs to the Web, it becomes clear that this language is well-suited for a wide range of applications and is an exciting area of exploration for developers.

articles.title: The Log Is the Agent
articles.summary: The Log Is the Agent describes a new runtime architecture that inverts traditional agent frameworks, where the log is the source of truth and the working graph is a deterministic projection. This design allows for deterministic replay, cheap branching, and end-to-end lineage tracing, making it suitable for self-improving agents and extending prior research on graph-memory systems.

articles.title: The Lion, The Witch, and the audacity of recruiters
articles.summary: The Lion, The Witch, and the audacity of recruiters has been on my mind lately. Two recruiters from a company with a history of questionable hiring practices have reached out to me about potential job openings. One offered a week-long trial contract for a Senior Elixir Developer position, which I was able to secure after some persistence and negotiation. The process was smooth, but the recruiter's audacity became apparent when they tried to get me to access their Slack workspace without my permission, claiming it needed to be contacted by an administrator. This experience highlights the lengths some recruiters will go to in order to get a candidate on board, even if it means exploiting loopholes and pushing them to work long hours for little reward.

articles.title: Why don't people use git properly?
articles.summary: People who write software for a living often don't use version control properly due to various reasons such as panic at merge conflicts, poor commit messages, and lack of knowledge about branching and pull requests. This can lead to issues like accidental data loss, incorrect updates, and compromised production databases. Understanding why this happens is useful, and it's likely that many developers struggle with using Git effectively despite its widespread adoption in the tech world.
